docs.title: Public DevOps Knowledge Base
docs.text:
This site contains sanitized DevOps standards, runbooks, templates, and anonymous migration cases.

The content is designed for two uses:

  • Human-readable operations and engineering documentation.
  • Retrieval-friendly source material for a DevOps Q&A assistant.

docs.title: Start Here
docs.text:

  • Docker Host Operations
  • Prometheus Alerting

docs.title: Publication Safety
docs.text: Only sanitized public documents are included in the site navigation. Internal audit files are not part of this published site.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard defines how shell scripts should be cleaned and published as reusable DevOps automation.

docs.title: Script Requirements
docs.text:
• Use set -euo pipefail where compatible.
  • Validate required commands.
  • Provide dry-run for destructive actions.
  • Log actions.
  • Avoid hardcoded credentials, IPs, domains, and personal paths.
  • Make thresholds configurable.

docs.title: Public Template
docs.text:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

main() {
  echo "run safe operation"
}

main "$@"

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard describes safe deployment and operation of Redash-style BI tools.

docs.title: Controls
docs.text:
• Protect admin access.
  • Store data source credentials securely.
  • Restrict query permissions.
  • Back up metadata database.
  • Review public dashboard sharing.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text: BI tools can expose production data through queries and dashboards. Do not publish data source credentials, query results, or customer data.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard describes how to operate Argo CD as a GitOps deployment controller for Kubernetes.

docs.title: GitOps Principles
docs.text:
1. Git is the desired-state source.
  2. Cluster changes should flow through reviewed commits.
  3. Secrets should be referenced through a secret management process, not stored in plain Git.
  4. Applications should have clear sync policies.
  5. Notifications should be routed without exposing webhook secrets.

docs.title: Installation Model
docs.text:
Use the official Argo CD manifests or Helm chart. For production, prefer high availability mode and separate the Argo CD namespace from application namespaces.

Validation:

kubectl get pods -n argocd
kubectl get svc -n argocd
kubectl get applications -n argocd

docs.title: Application Definition
docs.text:
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: Application
metadata:
  name: example-service
  namespace: argocd
spec:
  project: default
  source:
    repoURL: https://github.com/example/example-repo.git
    targetRevision: main
    path: deploy/kubernetes
  destination:
    server: https://kubernetes.default.svc
    namespace: example-namespace
  syncPolicy:
    automated:
      prune: false
      selfHeal: true

docs.title: Sync Policy Guidance
docs.text: Policy Use When Manual sync Production or high-risk services. Automated sync without prune Lower-risk services where deletion should remain manual. Automated sync with prune Mature services with strong review and rollback controls.

docs.title: Notification Standard
docs.text:
Notifications should include:

  • Application name.
  • Sync status.
  • Health status.
  • Revision.
  • Environment.
  • Link to runbook or dashboard.

Never commit notification webhook URLs or chat tokens in plain text.

docs.title: Backup and Restore
docs.text:
Export Argo CD application state:

argocd admin export > argocd-export.yaml

Store exports securely. Review exports before sharing because repository URLs, project names, and cluster references can reveal internal topology.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Symptom Check Application out of sync Git revision, generated manifests, ignored differences Sync fails RBAC, namespace existence, CRDs, admission policies Health unknown Resource health customization, CRD status fields Repo connection fails Repository credentials, network, known hosts

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard describes a safe Jenkins pipeline pattern for building container images and deploying to Kubernetes.

docs.title: Pipeline Stages
docs.text:
Use a predictable stage model:

checkout
test
build
scan
package
push image
deploy to staging
smoke test
manual approval
deploy to production
post-deploy verification

docs.title: Required Inputs
docs.text: Input Example Git repository https://github.com/example/example-repo.git Branch main Image registry.example.com/example/app Version Git SHA or release tag Kubernetes namespace example-namespace Deployment name example-service Environment staging or production

docs.title: Credential Handling
docs.text:
Store credentials in Jenkins credentials or an external secret manager:

  • Git credentials.
  • Registry credentials.
  • Kubernetes deploy credentials.
  • Notification webhook.
  • Cloud credentials.

Never hardcode credentials in Jenkinsfiles, shell scripts, or public documentation.

docs.title: Example Jenkinsfile Skeleton
docs.text:
pipeline {
  agent any

  environment {
    IMAGE = 'registry.example.com/example/app'
    NAMESPACE = 'example-namespace'
    DEPLOYMENT = 'example-service'
  }

  stages {
    stage('Checkout') {
      steps {
        checkout scm
      }
    }

    stage('Test') {
      steps {
        sh 'make test'
      }
    }

    stage('Build Image') {
      steps {
        sh 'docker build -t ${IMAGE}:${GIT_COMMIT} .'
      }
    }

    stage('Push Image') {
      steps {
        withCredentials([usernamePassword(credentialsId: 'registry-credentials', usernameVariable: 'REGISTRY_USER', passwordVariable: 'REGISTRY_PASSWORD')]) {
          sh 'echo "$REGISTRY_PASSWORD" | docker login registry.example.com -u "$REGISTRY_USER" --password-stdin'
          sh 'docker push ${IMAGE}:${GIT_COMMIT}'
        }
      }
    }

    stage('Deploy') {
      steps {
        sh 'kubectl -n ${NAMESPACE} set image deployment/${DEPLOYMENT} app=${IMAGE}:${GIT_COMMIT}'
        sh 'kubectl -n ${NAMESPACE} rollout status deployment/${DEPLOYMENT} --timeout=5m'
      }
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Kubernetes Deploy Safety
docs.text:
Before production deploy:

  • Confirm image tag is immutable.
  • Confirm Kubernetes context and namespace.
  • Confirm rollout strategy and readiness probes.
  • Confirm rollback command.
  • Confirm monitoring dashboard and alert ownership.

Rollback:

kubectl -n example-namespace rollout undo deployment/example-service
kubectl -n example-namespace rollout status deployment/example-service

docs.title: Notification Standard
docs.text:
Notifications should include:

  • Job name.
  • Build number.
  • Git commit.
  • Environment.
  • Deployment status.
  • Runbook link.

Do not include webhook URLs, credentials, or sensitive logs in notification templates.

docs.title: Cloud Network and Object Storage Migration Standard
Purpose
docs.text: This standard describes safe planning for cloud network connectivity and object storage migration across providers or accounts.

docs.title: Network Planning
docs.text:
Document:

  • Source and target VPC or VNet CIDRs.
  • Subnet layout.
  • Routing tables.
  • NAT and egress design.
  • Security groups or firewall policies.
  • DNS resolution.
  • Private connectivity such as VPN, peering, transit gateway, or cloud connect.

Use placeholder identifiers in public documents:

vpc-00000000000000000
subnet-00000000000000000
10.0.0.0/16
203.0.113.10

docs.title: Connectivity Validation
docs.text:
ping 10.0.0.10
traceroute 10.0.0.10
curl -I https://service.example.com
dig service.example.com

For private services, prefer application-level checks over raw ping.

docs.title: Object Storage Migration
docs.text:
Before migration:

  • Estimate object count and total size.
  • Check object naming and prefix strategy.
  • Validate permissions.
  • Decide metadata preservation requirements.
  • Plan incremental sync.
  • Define cutover and rollback.

Validation:

  • Object count comparison.
  • Size comparison.
  • Sample checksum comparison.
  • Application read test.
  • Access policy test.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Do not publish access keys or secret keys.
  • Use temporary roles where possible.
  • Restrict migration permissions to required buckets and prefixes.
  • Encrypt objects at rest and in transit.
  • Remove migration credentials after cutover.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard describes safe use of BuildKit for container image builds.

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Faster Dockerfile builds.
  • Build cache optimization.
  • Multi-platform image builds.
  • CI/CD image pipelines.

docs.title: Baseline
docs.text:
buildctl --version
docker buildx version

docs.title: Build Safety
docs.text:
• Do not bake secrets into image layers.
  • Use secret mounts for build-time credentials.
  • Pin base image versions.
  • Scan output images.
  • Push only to approved registries.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64 -t registry.example.com/example/app:1.0.0 .

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard describes safe operation of ChartMuseum or a similar private Helm chart repository.

docs.title: Controls
docs.text:
• Enable authentication.
  • Use TLS.
  • Back up chart storage.
  • Apply chart retention policies.
  • Restrict publishing permission.

docs.title: Validation

helm repo add example https://charts.example.com
helm search repo example

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard defines how to operate small or transitional service stacks with Docker Compose without publishing environment-specific secrets or runtime data.

docs.title: When To Use
docs.text: Use Docker Compose for development, demos, low-risk internal tools, or migration staging. Use Kubernetes or managed services for production workloads that require scheduling, autoscaling, or high availability.

docs.title: Stack Checklist
docs.text:
• Pin image versions.
  • Put persistent data in named volumes or documented bind mounts.
  • Add restart policies.
  • Configure log size limits.
  • Keep .env files out of public repositories.
  • Document backup and restore for every stateful service.
  • Review exposed ports.

docs.title: Compose Pattern
docs.text:
services:
  app:
    image: registry.example.com/example/app:1.0.0
    restart: unless-stopped
    env_file:
      - .env
    ports:
      - "8080:8080"
    logging:
      driver: json-file
      options:
        max-size: "100m"
        max-file: "3"

Do not publish the actual .env file. Publish .env.example with placeholder values only.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
docker compose config
docker compose ps
docker compose logs --tail=100 app

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text: • Docker Host Operations Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard describes installation, maintenance, cleanup, and recovery practices for Docker hosts.

docs.title: When To Use
docs.text: Use this standard for single-host Docker workloads, development hosts, lightweight internal services, and build machines. For production orchestration, prefer Kubernetes or another managed scheduler.

docs.title: Installation Principles
docs.text:
1. Install Docker from trusted repositories.
  2. Pin versions when reproducibility matters.
  3. Keep Docker data root on a volume with enough capacity.
  4. Configure logging limits before running long-lived containers.
  5. Avoid running business-critical services from unreviewed Compose files.

docs.title: Host Preparation
docs.text:
sudo mkdir -p /etc/docker
sudo mkdir -p /var/lib/docker

Recommended daemon configuration:

{
  "log-driver": "json-file",
  "log-opts": {
    "max-size": "100m",
    "max-file": "3"
  },
  "storage-driver": "overlay2"
}

Apply:

sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable --now docker
docker info

docs.title: Routine Maintenance
docs.text:
Check disk use:

docker system df
df -h /var/lib/docker

List resources:

docker ps -a
docker images
docker volume ls
docker network ls

Safe cleanup:

docker container prune -f
docker image prune -f
docker builder prune -f
docker network prune -f

Volume cleanup is high risk:

docker volume prune

Run volume cleanup only after confirming the volumes are not used by stateful applications.

docs.title: Docker Network Recovery
docs.text:
Symptoms of Docker network issues include containers failing to resolve names, bridge network conflicts, or services becoming unreachable after IP range changes.

Low-risk checks:

docker network ls
docker network inspect bridge
ip addr
iptables -S

Recovery sequence:

  1. Stop affected non-critical containers.
  2. Remove only unused Docker networks.
  3. Restart Docker.
  4. Recreate containers from reviewed Compose or deployment files.

Avoid deleting /var/lib/docker unless the host is disposable and all container state is backed up.

docs.title: Compose File Safety
docs.text:
Before running a Compose stack:

  • Review images and tags.
  • Replace hardcoded credentials with environment variables or secret references.
  • Confirm volumes and bind mounts.
  • Confirm exposed ports.
  • Add logging limits.
  • Add restart policies.
  • Document backup and restore for stateful services.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
docker version
docker info
docker run --rm hello-world
docker system df

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard describes safe operation of Harbor or similar private container registries.

docs.title: Core Controls
docs.text:
• Enable authentication.
  • Use project-level permissions.
  • Enable vulnerability scanning where available.
  • Configure retention policies.
  • Back up registry metadata and image storage.
  • Use TLS for all registry traffic.
  • Do not publish robot account tokens.

docs.title: Deployment Checklist
docs.text: Area Check TLS Certificate valid and renewal documented. Storage Object storage or persistent volume backed up. Access Admin account protected, robot accounts scoped. Retention Unused tags cleaned safely. Replication If used, credentials stored securely.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
docker login registry.example.com
docker pull registry.example.com/example/app:1.0.0
docker push registry.example.com/example/app:1.0.1

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard describes public-safe etcd operations for Kubernetes and distributed systems.

docs.title: Operating Principles
docs.text:
• Treat etcd as critical state.
  • Use an odd number of members for quorum.
  • Back up before cluster changes.
  • Monitor leader changes, disk latency, database size, and alarms.
  • Protect peer and client certificates.

docs.title: Backup
docs.text:
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save snapshot.db

Store snapshots encrypted and test restore regularly.

docs.title: Health Checks
docs.text:
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl endpoint health
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl endpoint status --write-out=table

docs.title: Kubernetes Notes
docs.text: For managed Kubernetes, control plane etcd is usually provider-managed. For self-managed clusters, etcd backup and restore must be part of the platform runbook.

docs.title: Publication Safety
docs.text: Do not publish real etcd certificates, keys, endpoints, snapshots, or cluster member names.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard describes safe MongoDB deployment, monitoring, backup, and migration practices.

docs.title: Baseline
docs.text:
• Enable authentication.
  • Bind only to private interfaces.
  • Use replica sets for availability.
  • Back up with a documented restore process.
  • Monitor connections, replication lag, locks, storage, and memory.

docs.title: Backup
docs.text:
mongodump --host mongo.example.internal --out /backup/mongodb

Store backups encrypted and test restore regularly.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: mongosh --host mongo.example.internal --eval 'rs.status()'

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text: Do not publish MongoDB connection strings, users, passwords, or internal hosts.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard defines public-safe MySQL installation, backup, migration, and recovery practices.

docs.title: Scope
docs.text: Use this for self-managed MySQL, cloud RDS-style MySQL, and migration planning. Provider-specific commands should be placed in cloud-specific appendices.

docs.title: Operating Principles
docs.text:
1. Prefer managed database services when operational maturity is limited.
  2. Separate administrative accounts from application accounts.
  3. Never publish initial root passwords, generated credentials, or connection strings.
  4. Test backup restore, not only backup creation.
  5. Use controlled maintenance windows for major upgrades and migrations.

docs.title: Installation Baseline
docs.text:
For self-managed MySQL:

  • Use a supported MySQL version.
  • Create a dedicated mysql system user.
  • Store data on a dedicated volume.
  • Set explicit character set and collation.
  • Configure slow query logging.
  • Enable service management through systemd.

Example configuration concepts:

[mysqld]
datadir=/var/lib/mysql
socket=/var/lib/mysql/mysql.sock
character-set-server=utf8mb4
collation-server=utf8mb4_unicode_ci
slow_query_log=ON
long_query_time=1
max_connections=500

docs.title: Account Model
docs.text:
Use least privilege:

CREATE USER 'app_user'@'10.0.0.%' IDENTIFIED BY '<REDACTED_PASSWORD>';
GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE ON example_db.* TO 'app_user'@'10.0.0.%';
FLUSH PRIVILEGES;

The password placeholder must be replaced through a secret manager, not committed to a repository.

docs.title: Backup Standard
docs.text:
Use both logical and physical backup when appropriate.

Logical backup:

mysqldump --single-transaction --routines --triggers --events example_db > example_db.sql

Physical backup tools are better for larger datasets. Always document:

  • Backup type.
  • Backup schedule.
  • Retention period.
  • Encryption method.
  • Restore test frequency.
  • Owner and escalation path.

docs.title: Migration Standard
docs.text:
Before migration:

  • Confirm source and target versions.
  • Check character set and collation.
  • Estimate data size and downtime.
  • Validate network throughput.
  • Freeze schema changes.
  • Prepare rollback plan.

Migration validation:

SELECT COUNT(*) FROM important_table;
SHOW SLAVE STATUS\G
SHOW REPLICA STATUS\G

Use checksums or application-level validation for critical tables.

docs.title: Root Password Reset
docs.text:
Root password reset is a break-glass procedure. It should require:

  • Approval.
  • Maintenance window.
  • Console or host-level access.
  • Audit record.
  • Immediate credential rotation after recovery.

Do not publish environment-specific reset passwords or internal access paths.

docs.title: Benchmarking
docs.text:
Use benchmarking to compare configurations, not to prove production capacity in isolation.

Example:

sysbench oltp_read_write --mysql-host=db.example.internal --mysql-user=example-user --mysql-db=example_db prepare
sysbench oltp_read_write --mysql-host=db.example.internal --mysql-user=example-user --mysql-db=example_db run

Provide the database password through a protected client configuration or secret injection mechanism rather than a command-line argument.

Avoid running benchmarks against production without explicit approval.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard defines installation, persistence, migration, and cluster practices for Redis.

docs.title: Operating Principles
docs.text:
1. Disable Transparent Huge Pages for Redis hosts.
  2. Set Linux overcommit and file descriptor limits deliberately.
  3. Protect Redis with network isolation and authentication.
  4. Choose RDB, AOF, or mixed persistence based on recovery requirements.
  5. Test migration and restore procedures before production use.

docs.title: Host Tuning
docs.text:
echo never | sudo tee /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled
echo 'vm.overcommit_memory = 1' | sudo tee /etc/sysctl.d/99-redis.conf
sudo sysctl --system

Set limits:

redis soft nofile 65535
redis hard nofile 65535

docs.title: Single Instance Baseline
docs.text:
Recommended configuration concepts:

bind 127.0.0.1 10.0.0.10
protected-mode yes
port 6379
dir /var/lib/redis
appendonly yes
appendfsync everysec
maxmemory-policy noeviction

Store credentials in a secret manager. Do not commit requirepass values.

docs.title: Persistence Choices
docs.text: Mode Use Case Trade-Off RDB Periodic snapshots Faster restart, possible data loss between snapshots. AOF Better durability Larger files and rewrite management required. Mixed Balanced durability and restore speed Requires version support and testing.

docs.title: AOF Migration Procedure
docs.text:
1. Confirm Redis version compatibility.
  2. Stop writes or enter a controlled maintenance window.
  3. Generate or rewrite AOF on the source.
  4. Copy AOF to the target through a secure channel.
  5. Verify checksum.
  6. Start target Redis.
  7. Validate keys, memory, persistence status, and application behavior.

Example checksum:

sha256sum appendonly.aof

docs.title: Cluster Migration Notes
docs.text:
For Redis Cluster:

  • Preserve slot ownership information.
  • Migrate one shard or replica group at a time.
  • Validate cluster_state:ok.
  • Confirm application clients support cluster topology refresh.
  • Keep rollback data until post-migration checks pass.

Validation:

redis-cli -h 10.0.0.10 -p 6379 INFO persistence
redis-cli -h 10.0.0.10 -p 6379 INFO replication
redis-cli -h 10.0.0.10 -p 6379 CLUSTER INFO

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Do not expose Redis directly to the public internet.
  • Restrict access by security group, firewall, or network policy.
  • Use TLS where supported and required.
  • Rotate credentials after migration or emergency access.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to decide whether a source file, note, diagram, runbook, script, or case from the Trash review workspace is valuable enough to become a public DevOps knowledge base article.

It is used for three goals:

  • Select high-value source material for the public DevOps knowledge base.
  • Rewrite internal or source-specific material into safe, reusable engineering guidance.
  • Prepare retrieval-friendly documents for a future DevOps Q&A assistant.

docs.title: Core Principle
docs.text:
A deep DevOps article should not only explain what to run. It should explain the operational problem, the constraints, the design choice, the trade-offs, the implementation path, the validation method, and the rollback strategy.

If a document only contains commands, raw configuration, screenshots, credentials, logs, or environment-specific notes, it is not a deep article by itself. It may still be useful as source material, but it must be rewritten, merged, or excluded.

docs.title: Publication Gate
docs.text:
No source material may become public content unless it passes all publication gates.

Gate Requirement Fail Result Public safety No secrets, keys, tokens, passwords, private certificates, internal domains, private IPs, customer names, personal data, private repositories, raw SQL dumps, production configs, or unreleased business details. Exclude or quarantine. Anonymization Project, customer, team, user, region, asset, domain, repository, account, and business names are replaced with generic placeholders. Rewrite before publication. Reusable value The material teaches a repeatable DevOps pattern, method, checklist, standard, or decision model. Merge into FAQ or exclude. Engineering context The article explains scenario, constraints, assumptions, and target state. Downgrade unless context can be reconstructed safely. Operational closure The article includes validation, rollback, risk, and maintenance guidance when operational change is involved. Rewrite before publication. Retrieval quality Headings are stable, sections are small, and each section answers one operational question. Restructure before publication.

docs.title: 1. Problem-First
docs.text:
The article begins with the operational problem, not the tool.

Good:

  • A cluster upgrade needs to reduce workload disruption.
  • A CI/CD process needs safer rollback and auditability.
  • A logging platform needs predictable retention and query performance.
  • A cloud network migration needs isolation, route control, and validation.

Weak:

  • How to install a tool without explaining why it matters.
  • A list of commands copied from a shell history.
  • A diagram with no explanation of constraints or target state.

docs.title: 2. Clear Scope And Boundaries
docs.text:
The article states when the guidance applies and when it does not.

Examples:

  • Applies to production Kubernetes clusters with multiple node groups.
  • Applies to single-host Docker workloads, not production orchestration.
  • Applies to log aggregation patterns, not application-level logging design.
  • Applies to cloud migration planning, not raw customer network replication.

docs.title: 3. Architecture Or Process Judgment
docs.text:
The article explains why a method is selected.

Examples:

  • Why use GitOps instead of ad hoc deployment scripts.
  • Why separate build, release, and deploy permissions.
  • Why prefer managed add-ons for EKS control plane integrations.
  • Why keep stateful data outside disposable compute nodes.

docs.title: 4. Trade-Offs
docs.text:
The article names the cost of the recommendation.

Common DevOps trade-offs:

  • Availability vs cost.
  • Standardization vs flexibility.
  • Automation vs manual control.
  • Managed services vs self-managed control.
  • Security isolation vs operational complexity.
  • Fast delivery vs release governance.

docs.title: 5. Implementation Path
docs.text:
The article provides a safe sequence:

  1. Preparation.
  2. Baseline capture.
  3. Change plan.
  4. Execution.
  5. Validation.
  6. Rollback.
  7. Handoff and maintenance.

docs.title: 6. Validation And Rollback
docs.text:
The article explains how to prove success and how to recover.

Validation should include:

  • What command, metric, log, dashboard, or user journey to check.
  • Expected healthy output or behavior.
  • Failure signals.
  • Post-change observation window.

Rollback should include:

  • What can be reverted.
  • What cannot be safely reverted.
  • What data or state must be preserved.
  • Who approves rollback in a production-like environment.

docs.title: 7. Security Boundary
docs.text:
The article treats security as part of the engineering design.

Security sections should cover relevant points:

  • Identity and access control.
  • Secret storage and rotation.
  • Network exposure and ingress.
  • CI/CD supply-chain risk.
  • Log and artifact leakage.
  • Backup and restore access.
  • Least privilege for automation.

docs.title: 8. Reusable Model
docs.text:
The article should produce a reusable model, not just a one-off story.

Examples of reusable models:

  • Kubernetes upgrade readiness model.
  • Deployment rollback decision model.
  • Observability onboarding model.
  • Cloud network migration validation model.
  • Secrets handling model for CI/CD.
  • Public documentation sanitization model.

docs.title: Standards
docs.text:
Use when the content defines a repeatable operating baseline.

Required sections:

  • Purpose.
  • When To Use.
  • Prerequisites.
  • Standard Procedure.
  • Validation.
  • Rollback or Recovery.
  • Security Notes.
  • FAQ.
  • Related Documents.

Examples:

  • Docker Host Operations Standard.
  • Kubernetes Cluster Provisioning Standard.
  • Terraform IaC Standard.

docs.title: Runbooks
docs.text:
Use when the content guides incident response or recurring operational action.

Required sections:

  • Symptom.
  • Impact.
  • Triage.
  • Decision Tree.
  • Remediation.
  • Validation.
  • Escalation.
  • Rollback.
  • Prevention.

Examples:

  • PVC Usage Alert Runbook.
  • TLS Certificate Expiry Runbook.
  • Deployment Rollback Runbook.

docs.title: Migration Cases
docs.text:
Use when the content comes from a project or architecture change and can be anonymized.

Required sections:

  • Anonymous Scenario.
  • Source And Target State.
  • Constraints.
  • Migration Strategy.
  • Phases.
  • Validation.
  • Rollback.
  • Risks.
  • Lessons Learned.
  • Reusable Checklist.

The case must be rewritten as a general standard approach. Do not preserve customer-specific topology, business workflow, domains, internal service names, cost tables, or private architecture details.

docs.title: Troubleshooting Articles
docs.text:
Use when the source explains a failure mode or recurring diagnostic pattern.

Required sections:

  • Symptom.
  • Scope.
  • Likely Causes.
  • Diagnostic Commands.
  • Evidence Interpretation.
  • Fix Options.
  • Validation.
  • Prevention.

docs.title: Automation Articles
docs.text:
Use when the source contains scripts or repeatable automation.

Required sections:

  • Problem Automated.
  • Inputs.
  • Outputs.
  • Required Permissions.
  • Failure Modes.
  • Dry Run Or Safe Mode.
  • Logging.
  • Rollback Or Manual Recovery.
  • Security Notes.

Never publish raw scripts that contain credentials, internal paths, production hostnames, account IDs, or private operational logic.

docs.title: Scoring Matrix
docs.text:
Score each candidate from 0 to 5.

Dimension 0 3 5 Problem value No clear problem. Useful but narrow operational problem. Common high-value DevOps problem with clear impact. Context completeness No scenario or assumptions. Some environment and constraints. Clear scope, constraints, target state, and non-goals. Engineering depth Commands only. Some reasoning and steps. Architecture/process judgment with trade-offs. Reusability One-off record. Can become a checklist or FAQ. Can become a standard, runbook, or model. Validation and rollback Missing. Basic validation only. Clear validation, failure signals, rollback, and maintenance. Security posture Risk unknown. Can be sanitized with effort. Cleanly anonymized with explicit safety guidance. Retrieval quality Hard to chunk or cite. Some useful headings. Stable headings, focused sections, direct Q&A value. Freshness Obsolete or version-locked. Needs version notes. Current or written as version-aware guidance.

docs.title: Rating Decision
docs.text:
Rating Rule Action A Score >= 32, security risk <= 2, and reusable value >= 4. Rewrite as a standalone public article. B Score 22-31, security risk <= 3, and at least one strong reusable section. Merge into an existing standard, runbook, FAQ, or checklist. C Score 12-21 or context is incomplete but some experience is useful. Keep as internal review note or extract a small sanitized snippet. D Score < 12, security risk >= 4, or mostly private/business/runtime material. Exclude, quarantine, archive, or delete according to cleanup policy.

Security risk is judged separately:

Security Risk Meaning 0 Public-safe generic material. 1 Minor identifiers that are easy to remove. 2 Some environment details requiring careful rewrite. 3 Heavy anonymization required; publish only if value is high. 4 Contains sensitive or private operational content; do not publish raw. 5 Contains credentials, keys, secrets, production data, private customer data, or personal data; quarantine or destroy.

docs.title: Source Review Output Format
docs.text:
When reviewing a file, use this format:

Conclusion: A/B/C/D
Recommended action: standalone article / merge / snippet / exclude / quarantine
Reason:
Security risk: 0-5
Scores:
- Problem value:
- Context completeness:
- Engineering depth:
- Reusability:
- Validation and rollback:
- Security posture:
- Retrieval quality:
- Freshness:
Potential public title:
Target directory:
Required anonymization:
Missing information:
Next step:

docs.title: Replace Specific Identifiers
docs.text:
Use placeholders:

<cluster-name>
<namespace>
<service-name>
<region>
<account-id>
<vpc-id>
<subnet-id>
<example.com>
<private-registry>
<repository-url>
<customer-name>
<team-name>

docs.title: Rewrite Raw Commands
docs.text:
Commands must be safe examples, not production history.

Bad:

kubectl config use-context real-prod-cluster
kubectl delete pod real-customer-payment-7d9d

Good:

kubectl config use-context <cluster-name>
kubectl -n <namespace> rollout status deployment/<deployment-name>

docs.title: Rewrite Architecture Cases
docs.text:
Do not publish original architecture diagrams, customer routes, service names, or network topology. Convert them into a generalized pattern:

  • Source state.
  • Target state.
  • Migration constraints.
  • Reusable design decision.
  • Validation checklist.

docs.title: Retrieval-Friendly Writing Rules
docs.text:
For Q&A assistant readiness:

  • Use descriptive headings.
  • Keep each section focused on one question or procedure.
  • Prefer short paragraphs and explicit lists.
  • Include definitions for ambiguous operational terms.
  • Avoid hidden assumptions.
  • Add validation and rollback sections even when short.
  • Link related documents using stable relative links.

Each article should answer likely questions such as:

  • When should I use this?
  • What are the prerequisites?
  • What is the safest implementation order?
  • How do I validate success?
  • How do I roll back?
  • What are the security risks?
  • What should I monitor after the change?

docs.title: Minimum Publication Checklist
docs.text:
Before a document is published:

  • It has a clear title and stable path.
  • It is linked from mkdocs.yml.
  • It contains no raw secrets, production data, or private identifiers.
  • It has public-safe placeholders.
  • It includes validation and rollback where relevant.
  • It includes security notes where relevant.
  • It builds successfully with MkDocs.
  • It is suitable for both human reading and retrieval-based Q&A.

docs.text: • Cloud Network and Object Storage Migration Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard describes a safe process for upgrading a managed or self-managed Kubernetes cluster. It is written for public reuse and does not depend on any private environment.

docs.title: When To Use
docs.text:
Use this procedure when:

  • Upgrading Amazon EKS, self-managed Kubernetes, or a Kubernetes-compatible platform.
  • Moving workloads from an older minor version to a newer supported minor version.
  • Replacing worker node groups during a platform upgrade.
  • Upgrading core add-ons such as CNI, CoreDNS, kube-proxy, ingress controllers, CSI drivers, monitoring, or logging agents.

docs.title: Upgrade Principles
docs.text:
1. Upgrade one minor version at a time unless the platform explicitly supports a larger jump.
  2. Treat add-ons as part of the cluster, not as optional extras.
  3. Upgrade control plane, add-ons, and nodes in a controlled sequence.
  4. Use new node groups for workload migration instead of in-place mutation when possible.
  5. Validate both Kubernetes health and application behavior before declaring the upgrade complete.
  6. Keep rollback paths available until post-upgrade monitoring is stable.

docs.title: Pre-Upgrade Assessment
docs.text:
Collect this baseline before making changes:

kubectl version --short
kubectl get nodes -o wide
kubectl get pods -A
kubectl get deployment,statefulset,daemonset -A
kubectl get ingress -A
kubectl get storageclass
kubectl get pvc -A
kubectl top nodes
kubectl top pods -A

Review these compatibility areas:

Area Check Kubernetes APIs Deprecated APIs removed in the target version. Add-ons CNI, CoreDNS, kube-proxy, CSI drivers, ingress controller, cert-manager, metrics-server. Workloads Pod disruption budgets, probes, resource requests, priority classes, security contexts. Storage CSI driver compatibility, PVC binding behavior, snapshot and backup support. Networking Ingress class behavior, load balancer annotations, service type changes, network policies. Observability Prometheus scrape targets, alert rules, dashboards, log collectors. Access IAM roles, RBAC, service accounts, cluster admin access, break-glass access.

docs.title: 1. Freeze Risky Changes
docs.text: Pause unrelated application deployments and infrastructure changes during the upgrade window. Communicate the maintenance window, expected impact, rollback criteria, and owner contacts.

docs.title: 2. Back Up Critical State
docs.text:
For managed clusters, export manifests and configuration:

kubectl get all -A -o yaml > cluster-workloads.snapshot.yaml
kubectl get configmap,ingress,svc,pvc,storageclass -A -o yaml > cluster-config.snapshot.yaml

Do not export raw Kubernetes Secret values into plain text files. Back up secrets through the platform-approved encrypted backup process or secret management system.

For self-managed clusters, back up etcd using the platform-recommended encrypted method.

docs.title: 3. Upgrade Control Plane
docs.text:
Upgrade the control plane through the cloud provider or cluster management tool. Wait until the API server is healthy and all core control plane checks pass.

Validation:

kubectl get --raw='/readyz?verbose'
kubectl get componentstatuses
kubectl get nodes

docs.title: 4. Upgrade Core Add-ons
docs.text:
Upgrade add-ons to versions compatible with the target Kubernetes version:

  • CNI plugin.
  • CoreDNS.
  • kube-proxy.
  • CSI drivers.
  • Metrics server.
  • Ingress controller.
  • Observability agents.

Validation:

kubectl get pods -n kube-system
kubectl rollout status deployment/coredns -n kube-system
kubectl get daemonset -n kube-system

docs.title: 5. Create New Worker Node Group
docs.text:
Create a new node group with the target Kubernetes-compatible image, runtime, IAM role, labels, taints, and scaling configuration.

Validation:

kubectl get nodes -L kubernetes.io/os,kubernetes.io/arch
kubectl describe node <new-node-name>

docs.title: 6. Drain Old Nodes Gradually
docs.text:
Drain old nodes in small batches:

kubectl cordon <old-node-name>
kubectl drain <old-node-name> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data --timeout=10m

After each batch, validate application health before continuing.

docs.title: 7. Validate Workloads
docs.text:
Check:

kubectl get pods -A
kubectl get events -A --sort-by='.lastTimestamp'
kubectl get ingress -A
kubectl get pvc -A

Application validation should include:

  • HTTP health checks.
  • Login or core API smoke tests.
  • Background job processing.
  • Database connection checks.
  • External callback or webhook checks.
  • Alerting and dashboard visibility.

docs.title: 8. Decommission Old Node Group
docs.text: After the observation window passes, scale down and remove the old node group. Keep snapshots and upgrade notes for review.

docs.title: Rollback Strategy
docs.text:
Managed Kubernetes control plane upgrades are often not directly reversible. Practical rollback usually means:

  • Stop node migration.
  • Keep or recreate old node groups if still supported.
  • Roll back add-ons to compatible versions when safe.
  • Roll back application deployments.
  • Restore from backups if stateful components are affected.

Define rollback triggers before starting:

  • Core business smoke test fails.
  • Error rate exceeds threshold for more than 10 minutes.
  • Critical pods cannot schedule.
  • Storage attach or mount errors affect stateful workloads.
  • Ingress or load balancer behavior breaks external traffic.

docs.title: Post-Upgrade Review
docs.text:
Record:

  • Source and target versions.
  • Add-on versions before and after.
  • Node group changes.
  • Incidents and mitigations.
  • Deprecated API findings.
  • Follow-up platform work.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard defines a repeatable process for creating Kubernetes clusters on managed platforms such as EKS and CCE, or self-managed platforms built with kubeadm.

docs.title: Provisioning Principles
docs.text:
1. Separate cluster planning from cluster creation.
  2. Use infrastructure as code or declarative configuration where possible.
  3. Keep network, IAM, storage, ingress, observability, and CI/CD requirements in the initial design.
  4. Install only required add-ons.
  5. Validate each platform layer before deploying applications.

docs.title: Planning Checklist
docs.text: Area Questions Version Which Kubernetes version is supported by the platform and add-ons? Network What VPC, subnets, service CIDR, pod CIDR, and ingress model are required? Nodes What instance types, architectures, labels, taints, and scaling rules are required? Storage Which CSI drivers and default StorageClasses are needed? Access Who has admin access, deploy access, and read-only access? Ingress Which ingress controller and load balancer mode will be used? Observability How will metrics, logs, dashboards, and alerts be deployed? CI/CD How will images be built, pushed, and deployed?

docs.title: Standard Add-on Order
docs.text:
Install and validate in this order:

  1. Core DNS and kube-proxy compatibility.
  2. CNI plugin.
  3. CSI drivers for block and shared storage.
  4. Metrics server.
  5. Ingress controller.
  6. Cluster autoscaler or managed node autoscaling.
  7. Observability stack.
  8. GitOps or CI/CD deploy tooling.
  9. Security policy and admission controls.

docs.title: Managed Cluster Creation Flow
docs.text:
Use the provider tool or infrastructure as code to create:

  • Cluster control plane.
  • Node groups.
  • IAM roles or workload identity.
  • Security groups or network policies.
  • Add-on versions.

Validation:

kubectl cluster-info
kubectl get nodes -o wide
kubectl get pods -A
kubectl get storageclass
kubectl get ingressclass

docs.title: Self-Managed kubeadm Flow
docs.text:
Before kubeadm init:

  • Disable swap.
  • Load bridge network modules.
  • Configure container runtime.
  • Configure system time.
  • Confirm hostnames and DNS.
  • Plan control plane endpoint for HA.

Validation:

kubeadm version
kubectl get nodes
kubectl get pods -n kube-system

docs.title: Application Readiness Gate
docs.text:
Do not deploy business applications until these checks pass:

  • Nodes are Ready.
  • System pods are healthy.
  • Metrics server returns data.
  • Default StorageClass is correct.
  • Ingress test endpoint works.
  • Cluster autoscaling behavior is understood.
  • Backup and restore process is documented.
  • Alert routing is tested.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard describes how to design and validate Kubernetes storage and ingress for public reusable guidance.

docs.title: Storage Classes
docs.text:
Use separate StorageClasses for different workload types:

Storage Type Typical Use Notes Block storage Databases and single-writer stateful workloads Usually ReadWriteOnce. Shared file storage Multi-pod shared files, uploads, reports Often ReadWriteMany. Ephemeral storage Cache or temporary processing Must tolerate data loss.

docs.title: CSI Driver Checklist
docs.text:
Before production use:

  • Driver version matches Kubernetes version.
  • IAM or workload identity is configured.
  • Node plugin pods are healthy.
  • Controller plugin pods are healthy.
  • Volume provisioning works.
  • Volume expansion works if required.
  • Snapshot support is tested if required.

Validation:

kubectl get csidriver
kubectl get pods -A | grep csi
kubectl get storageclass

docs.title: PVC Test
docs.text:
Create a test PVC and pod in a non-production namespace:

apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: example-pvc
  namespace: example-namespace
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 10Gi
  storageClassName: example-storage-class

Validate:

kubectl get pvc -n example-namespace
kubectl describe pvc example-pvc -n example-namespace

docs.title: Ingress Controller Checklist
docs.text:
Before exposing services:

  • IngressClass exists.
  • Controller pods are healthy.
  • Load balancer annotations are documented.
  • TLS certificate lifecycle is defined.
  • Default backend behavior is understood.
  • Access logs and metrics are enabled.
  • Health checks match application behavior.

Validation:

kubectl get ingressclass
kubectl get ingress -A
kubectl get svc -A | grep LoadBalancer

docs.title: Ingress Template
docs.text:
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: example-ingress
  namespace: example-namespace
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-body-size: "20m"
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  tls:
    - hosts:
        - service.example.com
      secretName: example-tls
  rules:
    - host: service.example.com
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: example-service
                port:
                  number: 80

Do not commit real TLS secrets.

docs.title: Common Failure Modes
docs.text: Area Symptom Check Storage PVC stuck pending StorageClass, CSI controller, IAM, quota Storage Pod mount failure Node plugin, filesystem, access mode Ingress 404 Ingress class, path, service selector Ingress 502 Backend pod readiness or service port Ingress TLS error Secret, certificate chain, host mismatch

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• NFS PVC Capacity Management
  • Nginx Reverse Proxy and TLS Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document defines a standard approach for monitoring and managing Kubernetes PersistentVolumeClaim capacity when NFS or NFS-like shared storage is used.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Kubernetes can show requested PVC capacity, but it does not always provide actual filesystem usage for NFS-backed paths. If usage is not monitored at the storage path level, applications can fail unexpectedly when the underlying shared filesystem fills up.

docs.title: Standard Approach
docs.text:
Use a two-layer model:

  1. Kubernetes metadata: namespace, PVC name, PV, StorageClass, requested size.
  2. Storage filesystem usage: actual path usage from the NFS server or mounted host.

The monitoring system should join both views and alert when usage crosses defined thresholds.

docs.title: Recommended Thresholds
docs.text: Level Usage Response Warning 75% Review growth rate and owner. High 85% Plan cleanup, expansion, or migration. Critical 90% Take immediate action and notify service owner. Emergency 95% Stop nonessential writes and execute expansion or cleanup.

docs.title: Required Metadata
docs.text:
Track these fields for each PVC:

cluster
namespace
pvc_name
pv_name
storage_class
requested_capacity
storage_path
actual_used
actual_available
usage_percent
owner
service
last_checked_at

docs.title: 1. Identify NFS StorageClasses
docs.text:
kubectl get storageclass
kubectl describe storageclass <storage-class-name>

Record provisioner, server, path pattern, reclaim policy, and expansion support.

docs.title: 2. List PVCs Using the StorageClass
docs.text:
kubectl get pvc -A -o wide
kubectl get pv

Map each PVC to its PV and storage path.

docs.title: 3. Measure Filesystem Usage
docs.text:
On the storage server or mounted observation host:

df -h <storage-root>
du -sh <storage-root>/<namespace>/<pvc-name>

Use a consistent path convention. If dynamic provisioning creates hashed paths, maintain a PV-to-path mapping.

docs.title: 4. Alert on Usage
docs.text:
Alert messages should include:

Cluster: example-cluster
Namespace: example-namespace
PVC: example-pvc
StorageClass: example-nfs
Usage: 87%
Threshold: 85%
Recommended Action: review growth and expand or clean up

Do not include credentials, real internal domains, or sensitive mount paths in public alert examples.

docs.title: 5. Remediate
docs.text:
Common remediation options:

  • Remove expired application files.
  • Archive old logs or attachments.
  • Expand the backing NFS volume.
  • Migrate a high-growth workload to a dedicated volume.
  • Add retention policies at application or storage level.

docs.title: Cleanup Safety
docs.text:
Never delete PVC data using a generic script unless all of these are true:

  • The owner approved the cleanup.
  • The target namespace and PVC are explicitly selected.
  • A backup or snapshot exists if data is business-critical.
  • The script has a dry-run mode.
  • The cleanup rule uses age, path, and file type filters.
  • The action logs every deleted object.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
After remediation:

df -h <storage-root>
du -sh <storage-root>/<namespace>/<pvc-name>
kubectl get pvc -n <namespace>
kubectl describe pvc <pvc-name> -n <namespace>

Verify the affected application can still read and write expected data.

docs.title: Why not rely only on kubectl get pvc?
docs.text: PVC capacity shows requested storage, not always real usage. NFS-backed storage requires filesystem-level observation.

docs.title: Should cleanup be automatic?
docs.text: Automatic cleanup is acceptable only for clearly disposable data such as temporary files. Business data should require owner approval or a policy-controlled retention system.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard defines a safe baseline for preparing Linux servers for DevOps workloads. It is intended for new servers, rebuilds, and post-provisioning hardening.

docs.title: Scope
docs.text: The baseline applies to common Linux distributions such as Rocky Linux, CentOS-compatible systems, Ubuntu, and Debian. Provider-specific steps should be handled in separate cloud build templates.

docs.title: Baseline Principles
docs.text:
1. Make changes idempotent where possible.
  2. Keep access control separate from application deployment.
  3. Prefer SSH key authentication over password login.
  4. Record every non-default system setting.
  5. Avoid embedding personal users, public keys, passwords, or internal mirrors in public scripts.
  6. Validate the server after each major configuration block.

docs.title: 1. Identify System
docs.text:
cat /etc/os-release
uname -a
hostnamectl

Record distribution, kernel, architecture, timezone, hostname, and primary network interface.

docs.title: 2. Update Packages
docs.text:
For RHEL-compatible systems:

sudo dnf makecache
sudo dnf update -y
sudo dnf install -y vim curl wget tar unzip net-tools lsof sysstat chrony bash-completion

For Debian-compatible systems:

sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get upgrade -y
sudo apt-get install -y vim curl wget tar unzip net-tools lsof sysstat chrony bash-completion

docs.title: 3. Configure Time Synchronization
docs.text:
sudo systemctl enable --now chronyd || sudo systemctl enable --now chrony
timedatectl
chronyc sources -v

Use the timezone required by the service owner. For public examples:

sudo timedatectl set-timezone UTC

docs.title: 4. Configure SSH Access
docs.text:
Recommended settings:

PermitRootLogin no
PasswordAuthentication no
PubkeyAuthentication yes
UseDNS no
GSSAPIAuthentication no

Validation:

sudo sshd -t
sudo systemctl reload sshd || sudo systemctl reload ssh

Never publish real SSH public keys or usernames in public scripts. Use placeholders such as example-user and ssh-ed25519 AAAA... devops@example.com.

docs.title: 5. Configure Command History
docs.text:
Recommended:

cat <<'EOF' | sudo tee /etc/profile.d/history-policy.sh
export HISTTIMEFORMAT='%F %T '
export HISTSIZE=10000
export HISTFILESIZE=20000
EOF

Do not rely on shell history as a security audit log. Use centralized audit logging for regulated systems.

docs.title: 6. Configure Kernel and Limits
docs.text:
Common baseline:

cat <<'EOF' | sudo tee /etc/sysctl.d/99-devops-baseline.conf
net.ipv4.ip_forward = 1
net.ipv4.tcp_tw_reuse = 1
vm.swappiness = 10
fs.file-max = 1048576
EOF

sudo sysctl --system

For service users:

cat <<'EOF' | sudo tee /etc/security/limits.d/99-devops-baseline.conf
* soft nofile 65535
* hard nofile 65535
* soft nproc 65535
* hard nproc 65535
EOF

docs.title: 7. Configure Firewall
docs.text:
Use a default-deny inbound model where possible. Open only documented ports.

For firewalld:

sudo systemctl enable --now firewalld
sudo firewall-cmd --get-active-zones
sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=ssh --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --reload

For cloud servers, align OS firewall rules with cloud security groups.

docs.title: 8. Prepare Data Disks
docs.text:
Before formatting a disk:

lsblk
blkid
df -hT

Only format disks that have been explicitly approved and are not mounted. See Linux Disk and LVM Expansion Runbook.

docs.title: Validation Checklist
docs.text:
• System packages updated.
  • Time sync enabled and healthy.
  • SSH config validates.
  • Firewall enabled or explicitly managed by cloud security groups.
  • Baseline sysctl settings applied.
  • Limits file installed where required.
  • Disk layout documented.
  • No plaintext credentials stored in bootstrap scripts.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Linux Disk and LVM Expansion Runbook
  • Docker Host Operations Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard summarizes safe operating practices for Kubernetes API gateways such as APISIX, Kong, or Nginx-based ingress gateways.

docs.title: Gateway Responsibilities
docs.text:
An API gateway commonly handles:

  • Routing.
  • TLS termination.
  • Authentication and authorization integration.
  • Rate limiting.
  • Request and response transformation.
  • Observability.
  • Canary or weighted routing.

docs.title: Deployment Checklist
docs.text:
Before production use:

  • Gateway controller is highly available.
  • Data plane pods have readiness and liveness probes.
  • Admin API is not publicly exposed.
  • Dashboard access is protected.
  • TLS certificate lifecycle is documented.
  • Metrics are scraped by Prometheus.
  • Access logs are routed to the logging platform.
  • Rate limiting and timeout defaults are defined.

docs.title: Route Template
docs.text:
Use placeholders in public examples:

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: example-gateway-route
  namespace: example-namespace
spec:
  ingressClassName: example-gateway
  rules:
    - host: api.example.com
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /v1
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: example-api
                port:
                  number: 80

docs.title: Security Controls
docs.text:
• Disable anonymous admin access.
  • Restrict admin endpoints by network policy.
  • Store admin keys in a secret manager.
  • Use separate routes for internal and external APIs.
  • Enforce request size and timeout limits.
  • Enable audit logging for route changes.

docs.title: Observability
docs.text:
Minimum metrics:

  • Request rate.
  • Error rate.
  • Latency percentiles.
  • Upstream health.
  • Gateway pod CPU and memory.
  • Route-level 4xx and 5xx counts.

docs.title: Change Procedure
docs.text:
1. Review route manifest.
  2. Validate service and port.
  3. Apply to staging.
  4. Run smoke test.
  5. Apply to production.
  6. Confirm metrics and logs.
  7. Record rollback command.

Rollback:

kubectl -n example-namespace rollout undo deployment/example-api
kubectl -n example-namespace delete ingress example-gateway-route

docs.text:
• Kubernetes Storage and Ingress Standard
  • Prometheus Alerting Standard

docs.title: Operational Areas
docs.text:
• Metadata database backup.
  • Worker capacity.
  • Task retry policy.
  • Alerting.
  • Permission model.

docs.title: Validation

• Scheduler process healthy.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard describes public-safe deployment, migration, and validation practices for Kafka and ZooKeeper.

docs.title: Scope
docs.text: Use this for legacy ZooKeeper-backed Kafka clusters and migration planning. For new systems, evaluate modern Kafka modes and managed streaming services.

docs.title: Planning Checklist
docs.text: Area Questions Brokers How many brokers and what storage class? ZooKeeper Is ZooKeeper still required by the Kafka version? Topics What partitions, replication factors, and retention policies are required? Clients Can producers and consumers tolerate rolling restarts? Security Are authentication, authorization, and TLS required? Observability Are broker, topic, consumer lag, and JVM metrics collected?

docs.title: Deployment Principles
docs.text:
1. Keep broker IDs stable.
  2. Store data on dedicated persistent volumes.
  3. Use replication factor greater than one for critical topics.
  4. Monitor under-replicated partitions and consumer lag.
  5. Test producer and consumer behavior before production cutover.

docs.title: Migration Flow
docs.text:
1. Freeze topic topology changes.
  2. Capture topic list, partition count, replication factor, and consumer groups.
  3. Deploy target cluster.
  4. Configure secure connectivity between source and target.
  5. Mirror or copy data using an approved migration tool.
  6. Validate topic offsets and consumer lag.
  7. Cut over producers.
  8. Cut over consumers.
  9. Keep source cluster read-only until rollback window closes.

docs.title: Validation Commands
docs.text:
kafka-topics.sh --bootstrap-server broker.example.com:9092 --list
kafka-consumer-groups.sh --bootstrap-server broker.example.com:9092 --all-groups --describe
kafka-topics.sh --bootstrap-server broker.example.com:9092 --describe --topic example-topic

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text: Rollback depends on whether producers have written to the target. Define the cutoff point before migration. If bidirectional writes are not supported, rollback requires stopping target writes and restoring producer routing to the source.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text: • Prometheus Exporter Monitoring Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard describes how to operate Nacos as a configuration and service discovery component.

docs.title: Deployment Principles
docs.text:
• Use a cluster for production.
  • Store metadata in a managed or highly available database.
  • Restrict console access.
  • Back up configuration data.
  • Monitor JVM, database connectivity, and service registration health.

docs.title: Kubernetes Checklist
docs.text:
kubectl get statefulset -n example-namespace
kubectl get svc -n example-namespace
kubectl logs -n example-namespace statefulset/example-nacos

docs.title: Risk Areas
docs.text:
• Configuration changes can affect many services.
  • Database credentials must be secret-managed.
  • Console access should not be public.

docs.title: Related Documents

docs.title: Case Type
docs.text: Traditional service to container platform migration.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: A legacy application stack needed to move from manually managed hosts to a containerized deployment model. The original diagrams and service names are not published.

docs.title: Standard Migration Model
docs.text:
1. Inventory services, ports, dependencies, data paths, and startup order.
  2. Build container images with pinned base images.
  3. Externalize configuration.
  4. Move credentials into secret management.
  5. Define health checks.
  6. Deploy to staging.
  7. Run smoke tests.
  8. Cut over traffic gradually.
  9. Keep rollback path to the original deployment until stable.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
• Container starts reliably.
  • Logs are available.
  • Health endpoint passes.
  • Database and cache connections work.
  • Ingress or gateway route works.
  • Monitoring and alerts cover the new deployment.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Docker Compose Stack Standard
  • Kubernetes Cluster Provisioning Standard

docs.title: Case Type
docs.text: Kubernetes platform upgrade and workload migration.

docs.title: Background
docs.text:
An organization operated a Kubernetes cluster running an older minor version. The cluster hosted multiple stateless services, ingress routes, monitoring components, and stateful dependencies. The platform needed to move to a newer supported version while minimizing service interruption.

All identifiers in this case are generic. No original service names, domains, regions, accounts, repositories, or customer details are preserved.

docs.title: Challenges
docs.text:
• The target Kubernetes version required add-on compatibility checks.
  • Some workloads depended on ingress and load balancer behavior.
  • Stateful components used persistent storage.
  • Monitoring and alerting needed to remain available throughout the change.
  • Old worker nodes could not be replaced all at once without risking capacity pressure.

docs.title: Standard Solution
docs.text:
The upgrade was modeled as a phased migration:

  1. Build a baseline inventory of workloads, nodes, ingress, PVCs, add-ons, and alerts.
  2. Freeze unrelated infrastructure changes during the upgrade window.
  3. Upgrade the control plane using the platform-supported method.
  4. Upgrade core add-ons to versions compatible with the target Kubernetes version.
  5. Create a new worker node group with the target runtime and image.
  6. Migrate workloads by cordoning and draining old nodes in small batches.
  7. Validate application health after each batch.
  8. Keep old nodes available until the observation window passes.
  9. Remove old nodes after metrics and business checks remain stable.

docs.title: Validation Model
docs.text:
The team used three validation layers:

Layer Examples Cluster health API readiness, node readiness, system pods, events. Workload health Pod readiness, rollout status, logs, probes, job completion. Business smoke tests Login, core API calls, scheduled jobs, external callbacks, dashboard visibility.

docs.title: Rollback Model
docs.text:
The control plane upgrade itself was treated as forward-only. Rollback protection came from:

  • Keeping old worker nodes until workload validation passed.
  • Migrating workloads gradually.
  • Preserving manifest snapshots.
  • Maintaining application rollback ability.
  • Avoiding irreversible storage changes during the same window.

docs.title: Reusable Lessons
docs.text:
• Add-on compatibility often causes more practical risk than the control plane version change.
  • New node groups provide a cleaner rollback boundary than in-place node mutation.
  • Ingress, CSI drivers, and monitoring agents deserve explicit test cases.
  • PVC capacity should be checked before migration to avoid confusing upgrade issues with storage pressure.
  • Every critical alert should link to a runbook before the maintenance window.

docs.title: Related Standards
docs.text:
• EKS and Kubernetes Upgrade Standard
  • NFS PVC Capacity Management
  • Prometheus Alerting Standard

docs.title: Case Type
docs.text: Cross-provider object storage migration.

docs.title: Background
docs.text:
An engineering team needed to move application files from one object storage provider to another while preserving application availability and minimizing data inconsistency.

All identifiers are generic. No original bucket names, domains, regions, customer data, or access credentials are preserved.

docs.title: Standard Solution
docs.text:
The migration used an incremental sync model:

  1. Inventory source buckets and prefixes.
  2. Estimate object count and total size.
  3. Create target buckets and access policies.
  4. Run initial bulk sync.
  5. Compare object counts and sample checksums.
  6. Run incremental sync for changed objects.
  7. Switch application reads to target storage.
  8. Switch application writes to target storage.
  9. Keep source storage read-only during rollback window.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
• Object count comparison.
  • Total size comparison.
  • Sample checksum validation.
  • Application read smoke test.
  • Upload smoke test.
  • Access policy review.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text: Rollback is easiest before write traffic moves to the target. After target writes begin, rollback requires either reverse sync or application-level reconciliation.

docs.title: Reusable Lessons
docs.text:
• Count and size checks are not enough; sample checksums and application reads are needed.
  • Write cutover is the true point of risk.
  • Temporary credentials should be removed immediately after migration.
  • Prefix mapping must be reviewed before the first sync.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text: • Cloud Network and Object Storage Migration Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard defines public-safe HAProxy reverse proxy and load balancing practices.

docs.title: Basic Pattern
docs.text:
frontend example_http
  bind *:80
  default_backend example_backend

backend example_backend
  balance roundrobin
  option httpchk GET /health
  server app1 10.0.0.10:8080 check
  server app2 10.0.0.11:8080 check

docs.title: Operational Checks
docs.text:
haproxy -c -f /etc/haproxy/haproxy.cfg
systemctl reload haproxy
systemctl status haproxy --no-pager

docs.title: Safety Notes
docs.text:
• Do not publish real backend addresses.
  • Use health checks that match application readiness.
  • Use TLS termination only with managed certificate lifecycle.
  • Restrict stats page access.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text: • Nginx Reverse Proxy and TLS Standard

docs.title: Nginx Reverse Proxy and TLS Standard
Purpose
docs.text: This standard defines safe public patterns for deploying Nginx or OpenResty as a reverse proxy with TLS.

docs.title: Core Principles
docs.text:
1. Separate public listener configuration from upstream service configuration.
  2. Use TLS certificates from a controlled lifecycle process.
  3. Do not publish real certificates, private keys, domains, or upstream addresses.
  4. Use health checks and reload validation before applying changes.
  5. Keep access logs useful while avoiding sensitive request data where possible.

docs.title: Basic Reverse Proxy Template
docs.text:
server {
    listen 80;
    server_name service.example.com;

    location / {
        proxy_pass http://example-upstream;
        proxy_set_header Host $host;
        proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr;
        proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for;
        proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme;
        proxy_connect_timeout 5s;
        proxy_send_timeout 60s;
        proxy_read_timeout 60s;
    }
}

Upstream example:

upstream example-upstream {
    server 10.0.0.10:8080 max_fails=3 fail_timeout=30s;
    server 10.0.0.11:8080 max_fails=3 fail_timeout=30s;
}

docs.title: TLS Template
docs.text:
server {
    listen 443 ssl http2;
    server_name service.example.com;

    ssl_certificate /etc/nginx/certs/service.example.com.crt;
    ssl_certificate_key /etc/nginx/certs/service.example.com.key;
    ssl_protocols TLSv1.2 TLSv1.3;
    ssl_prefer_server_ciphers on;

    location / {
        proxy_pass http://example-upstream;
        proxy_set_header Host $host;
        proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr;
        proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for;
        proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto https;
    }
}

The key path is illustrative. Never commit actual private key files.

docs.title: Certificate Expiry Check
docs.text:
echo | openssl s_client -servername service.example.com -connect service.example.com:443 2>/dev/null \
  | openssl x509 -noout -dates

For local files:

openssl x509 -in /etc/nginx/certs/service.example.com.crt -noout -dates

docs.title: Reload Procedure
docs.text:
sudo nginx -t
sudo systemctl reload nginx
sudo systemctl status nginx --no-pager

Validation:

curl -I https://service.example.com
curl -sS https://service.example.com/health

docs.title: Common Failure Modes
docs.text: Symptom Likely Cause Check 502 Bad Gateway Upstream unavailable or wrong port curl upstream from proxy host TLS warning Expired or wrong certificate openssl x509 -noout -dates Redirect loop Incorrect forwarded protocol Check X-Forwarded-Proto handling Large upload fails Body size too small client_max_body_size Long request times out Proxy timeout too low proxy_read_timeout

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Store private keys with restrictive permissions.
  • Use automation for renewal where possible.
  • Test renewal in staging before production.
  • Avoid logging authorization headers.
  • Do not expose admin routes through public listeners unless explicitly required and protected.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text: • Prometheus Alerting Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard defines safe operational practices for OpenVPN-based administrative access.

docs.title: Principles
docs.text:
• Use VPN for administrative access, not as a substitute for least privilege.

  • Revoke unused certificates.
  • Restrict routes to required networks.
  • Log connections without exposing private keys.

docs.title: Operational Checklist
docs.text:
• Server certificate is valid.
  • Client certificates are tracked.
  • Revocation list is active.
  • Firewall allows only required VPN traffic.
  • Admin access is logged.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text: Never publish VPN profiles, private keys, user certificates, or shared secrets.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard defines how to add Prometheus exporters for services such as MySQL, Redis, MongoDB, Nginx, JVM applications, Node Exporter, and blackbox monitoring.

docs.title: Exporter Principles
docs.text:
1. Run exporters close to the target service.
  2. Use least-privilege metrics accounts.
  3. Avoid exposing exporters publicly.
  4. Add labels that identify service, environment, and owner.
  5. Pair every exporter with dashboards and alert rules.

docs.title: Exporter Checklist
docs.text: Exporter Key Checks Node Exporter CPU, memory, disk, filesystem, network. MySQL Exporter Connection, replication, slow queries, buffer pool. Redis Exporter Memory, clients, persistence, replication, keyspace. MongoDB Exporter Connections, locks, replication, storage. Nginx Exporter Requests, connections, status endpoint. JVM Exporter Heap, GC, threads, class loading. Blackbox Exporter HTTP, TCP, DNS, TLS expiry.

docs.title: Scrape Configuration Pattern
docs.text:
scrape_configs:
  - job_name: example-service
    static_configs:
      - targets:
          - exporter.example.internal:9100
        labels:
          service: example-service
          team: platform

Do not publish real internal targets.

docs.title: Alert Coverage
docs.text:
Minimum alert categories:

  • Exporter down.
  • Target down.
  • Disk capacity.
  • Memory pressure.
  • Error rate.
  • Replication lag.
  • TLS expiry.
  • JVM GC pressure.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
curl -sS http://exporter.example.internal:9100/metrics | head

In Prometheus:

up{job="example-service"}

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Exporters may expose service metadata.
  • Restrict exporter ports to Prometheus.
  • Use TLS or private networking where required.
  • Store database metrics credentials in a secret manager.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text: • Prometheus Alerting Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard defines a safe baseline for collecting Kubernetes application logs with Loki-compatible tooling.

docs.title: Logging Principles
docs.text:
1. Logs should support troubleshooting without exposing secrets.
  2. Labels should be low-cardinality.
  3. Retention should match compliance and cost requirements.
  4. Application logs should use structured fields where possible.
  5. Sensitive fields must be filtered before storage.

docs.title: Label Guidance
docs.text:
Recommended labels:

cluster
namespace
pod
container
app
environment
team

Avoid labels such as request ID, user ID, email, order ID, or full URL because they create high cardinality or privacy risk.

docs.title: Kubernetes Collection Flow
docs.text:
1. Deploy log collector as a DaemonSet.
  2. Collect container logs from node log paths.
  3. Add Kubernetes metadata.
  4. Drop sensitive or high-cardinality labels.
  5. Send logs to Loki.
  6. Validate queries in Grafana.

docs.title: Validation Queries
docs.text:
{namespace="example-namespace"}
{app="example-service"} |= "error"

docs.title: Retention and Cost
docs.text:
Define:

  • Default retention.
  • Extended retention for critical services.
  • Excluded namespaces.
  • Log volume budget.
  • Query performance expectations.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Do not log credentials, tokens, session IDs, or full authorization headers.
  • Mask personal data at application or collector layer.
  • Restrict access to logs by team and environment.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Prometheus Alerting Standard
  • Publication Safety Checklist

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard defines basic Node Exporter deployment and alert coverage.

docs.title: Metrics To Use
docs.text:
• CPU saturation.
  • Memory pressure.
  • Disk capacity.
  • Filesystem errors.
  • Network errors.
  • Load average.

docs.title: Deployment Notes
docs.text:
Run Node Exporter on each Linux host and restrict access to Prometheus.

Validation:

curl -sS http://node.example.internal:9100/metrics | head

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text: • Prometheus Exporter Monitoring Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard defines how to write, review, and operate Prometheus alerts for public reusable DevOps documentation.

docs.title: Alert Design Principles
docs.text:
1. Alert only on symptoms that need human action.
  2. Use warning and critical levels with different urgency.
  3. Include service, environment, owner, and runbook labels.
  4. Avoid alerting on short transient spikes unless the service requires it.
  5. Every critical alert must have a runbook.
  6. Alert messages must be safe to share and must not include secrets.

docs.title: Recommended Labels
docs.text:
labels:
  severity: warning
  team: platform
  service: example-service
  runbook: kubernetes-pvc-usage-alert-runbook

docs.title: Recommended Annotations
docs.text:
annotations:
  summary: "PVC usage is above warning threshold"
  description: "PVC {{ $labels.persistentvolumeclaim }} in namespace {{ $labels.namespace }} is using {{ $value }} percent of available capacity."
  action: "Check recent growth, confirm owner, and follow the PVC usage runbook."

docs.title: Example Rule: PVC Capacity
docs.text:
groups:
  - name: kubernetes-storage
    rules:
      - alert: KubernetesPVCUsageHigh
        expr: kubelet_volume_stats_used_bytes / kubelet_volume_stats_capacity_bytes * 100 > 85
        for: 15m
        labels:
          severity: warning
          team: platform
          service: storage
          runbook: kubernetes-pvc-usage-alert-runbook
        annotations:
          summary: "PVC usage is above 85%"
          description: "PVC {{ $labels.persistentvolumeclaim }} in namespace {{ $labels.namespace }} is above 85% usage for 15 minutes."
          action: "Review growth, confirm owner, and prepare cleanup or expansion."
      - alert: KubernetesPVCUsageCritical
        expr: kubelet_volume_stats_used_bytes / kubelet_volume_stats_capacity_bytes * 100 > 90
        for: 10m
        labels:
          severity: critical
          team: platform
          service: storage
          runbook: kubernetes-pvc-usage-alert-runbook
        annotations:
          summary: "PVC usage is above 90%"
          description: "PVC {{ $labels.persistentvolumeclaim }} in namespace {{ $labels.namespace }} is above 90% usage for 10 minutes."
          action: "Execute the PVC usage runbook and notify the service owner."

docs.title: Alert Review Checklist
docs.text:
Before enabling a new alert:

  • The alert has a clear owner.
  • The expression has been tested in Prometheus.
  • The threshold is based on operational risk, not arbitrary values.
  • The for duration filters normal noise.
  • The alert includes a runbook label.
  • The message does not expose secrets, real internal domains, or customer data.
  • The notification route matches severity.
  • The alert has been observed in a staging or test environment when possible.

docs.title: Notification Routing
docs.text:
Use this model:

Severity Route Expected Response info Dashboard or low-priority channel Review during business hours. warning Team notification channel Acknowledge and plan action. critical On-call channel Immediate response.

Public examples should use placeholder channels such as platform-alerts and never include webhook URLs.

docs.title: Common Anti-Patterns
docs.text:
• Alerting on every pod restart without context.
  • Using critical severity for capacity warning.
  • Embedding real hostnames or domains in alert examples.
  • Creating alerts without runbooks.
  • Alerting on metrics that disappear during outages without using absent() where appropriate.

docs.title: Application Maintenance and Health Check Standard
Purpose
docs.text: This standard defines a reusable model for application health checks and maintenance runbooks.

docs.title: Health Check Layers
docs.text: Layer Examples Infrastructure Host, CPU, memory, disk, network. Runtime Process, container, pod, JVM, worker. Application HTTP health endpoint, queue consumer, scheduler. Dependency Database, cache, object storage, third-party API. User Journey Login, core API call, report generation, callback.

docs.title: Maintenance Workflow
docs.text:
1. Announce maintenance window.
  2. Confirm backup and rollback.
  3. Pause risky automated jobs.
  4. Apply change.
  5. Run technical validation.
  6. Run business smoke tests.
  7. Monitor for the observation window.
  8. Close with summary and follow-up items.
